patches.find:
2.3 Gravity at the Rim: 90% Drop

The dome's exponential profile g(r) = g₀ × exp(−r/8,619) predicts gravity drops to ~10% of normal at the Antarctic barrier (r ≈ 20,015 km). Measured gravity at southern stations varies by less than 0.5% from expected values. The formula the dome uses for other predictions contradicts observation by a factor of ten at the rim.

The dome's geometry: Aetheric circulation in a toroidal loop, with "circulating aether" providing local gravity. The dome's own exponential profile gives g(r) = g₀ × exp(−r / 8,619 km), predicting gravity decreases exponentially with distance from the north pole.

The problem: The dome's exponential gravity formula predicts a dramatic drop: at r = 6,000 km (roughly South Africa), gravity has already fallen by 50%. At the ice wall (r = 20,015 km), g drops to roughly 10% of its polar value — a 90% reduction. This is not a minor discrepancy. Measurements show gravity varies by only 0.53% across the entire Earth's surface, from ~9.78 m/s² at the equator to ~9.83 m/s² at the poles. The dome's geometry predicts a variation 170 times larger than observed, in the wrong direction (decreasing southward rather than increasing).

How the dome resolves it: The author does not calculate gravity from aetheric circulation. Instead, the model page and source code use the WGS84 empirical surface gravity formula: g = 9.7803 × (1 + 0.00527 × sin²φ + 0.0000233 × sin⁴φ) m/s², or equivalently the Somigliana form g ≈ 9.7803 − 0.0325 × cos(2φ). This formula is derived from an oblate rotating sphere — it encodes the Earth's equatorial bulge, polar flattening, and centrifugal acceleration due to rotation. None of these quantities exist on a flat disc. The dome's gravity "prediction" is the globe's gravity formula with the label changed (see also Section 2.8, which traces the same formula in the source code).
patches.replace:
Link fixed: true Old link gone: true

2.3 Gravity at the Rim: 90% Drop

The dome's own exponential height profile H(r) drops from 8,537 km at the pole to 837 km at the rim — a 10:1 ratio. If gravity comes from the aetheric column, this predicts a dramatic ~90% gravity reduction at southern latitudes. Measured gravity varies by only 0.5%. If gravity does NOT come from the aetheric column, the dome's gravity WINs have no physical basis. Either way, the model fails.

The dome's geometry: The model's exponential firmament profile H(r) = 8,537 × exp(−r/8,619) km gives H ≈ 8,537 km at the north pole (r = 0) and H ≈ 837 km at the Antarctic barrier (r = 20,015 km) — a factor of 10.2. Any physical model where gravity arises from the aetheric column overhead predicts gravitational acceleration should track this ratio. At r = 6,000 km (roughly South Africa), the column has already dropped by half. At the rim, it is at 10% of its polar value.

The measurements: Gravity at the North Pole (~9.832 m/s²) and the South Pole (~9.832 m/s²) are nearly identical. Across the entire Earth's surface, gravity varies by only 0.53% — from ~9.780 m/s² at the equator to ~9.832 m/s² at the poles. The dome's H(r) predicts a 10:1 variation; reality shows a 1.005:1 variation. The prediction overshoots observation by a factor of approximately 170. (See WIN-067 for the full quantitative derivation.)

The dilemma: The dome model faces an unavoidable fork. If gravity arises from the aetheric pressure column — the only mechanism the dome's architecture provides — then H(r) varying by 10:1 should produce dramatic, easily measurable gravity variation that does not exist. If gravity does NOT arise from the aetheric column, then the dome has no gravity mechanism at all, and WIN-011, WIN-012, WIN-013, WIN-014, and WIN-067 (all gravity-related claims) have no dome-derived physical basis.

How the dome resolves it: The model page and source code use g(r) = 9.7803 × (1 + 0.005307 × e−r/8619) m/s². This formula borrows 9.7803 m/s² — the observed equatorial gravity from the WGS84 ellipsoid model — as its starting value, then modulates it using the dome's exponential decay constant λg = 8,619 km and a coefficient (0.005307) close to WGS84's ellipsoidal flattening factor (0.00527). The result approximates WGS84 values at moderate latitudes by construction: the starting value is the observed answer, and the exponential term is fitted to reproduce the observed latitude variation. This is not a prediction derived from dome geometry — it is a curve fit to globe-derived data, relabeled as a dome formula. (See also Section 2b, which traces the same formula in the source code.)
patches.rationale: EXP-121: Replace SEC-2.3 (Gravity at the Rim) with improved version that (1) corrects formula attribution from WGS84 directly to dome actual formula g(r)=9.7803x(1+0.005307xe^(-r/8619)), (2) adds dilemma framing (aetheric column or no mechanism), (3) adds H(r) pole/rim computation and WIN-067 cross-reference. Analyst-authored expansion addressing ISS-806.
